9ed75ad0-3e86-4a07-84d0-f1b022eafc3f.titulo: Introduction to the craft: art, science and responsibility of the effects operator
9ed75ad0-3e86-4a07-84d0-f1b022eafc3f.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Before any spark, there is a trained, calm and disciplined professional. That professional is what this course is about.

Pyrotechnics and special stage effects bring together chemistry, physics, engineering, art and — above all — risk management. This lesson defines what the effects operator is (also called special effects technician or pyrotechnician), the scope of the profession, and why safety is the central axis of everything we do in the PLENO EFEITO course. You are not just someone who 'presses the button': you are the technical head of a high-risk operation involving the audience, artists, structures and property.

  • What the effects operator is: The professional who designs, sets up, operates and dismantles visual and sensory effects at live events — fireworks, cold sparks, CO2 jets, smoke, controlled fire, confetti and streamers, panels and lighting. The craft combines artistic sensibility (creating emotion and impact) with technical rigor (chemistry of compounds, safety distances, synchronization). Above all, it is a role of legal responsibility: the signature of the technical head answers for incidents.
  • The fusion of science and creativity: Every effect is the result of physical-chemical principles: oxidizers and fuels generating light and heat, pressurized gases producing clouds, pyrotechnic delays defining timing. The operator translates an artistic idea ('I want a golden cascade falling from the ceiling on the chorus') into safe technical parameters (which product, which distance, which angle, which cue).
  • Safety first — always: Pyrotechnics is classified as a HIGH-RISK activity because it handles combustible and explosive materials, pressurized gases and intense heat sources. No effect is worth an accident. Safety culture is not an add-on to the course: it is the course. Every creative decision passes through the filter 'is this safe for the audience, crew, artists and venue?'.
  • Mastery of temporal synchronization: Timing is a critical variable. A firing off-time ruins the impact and can be dangerous. The operator is the 'temporal conductor' who syncs effects with music, light and video — manually or via time code. Understanding the delays inherent to each product is an essential part of planning.
  • Teamwork and chain of command: Effects are never a solo operation. There is dialogue with production, stage direction, sound design, lighting, event security, the fire brigade and firefighters. Clear communication, agreed cues and a hierarchy of decision (who aborts the show?) are as important as the equipment.
  • Ethics and compliance: The professional operator never improvises on safety, never operates without a license, never uses an expired or non-compliant product, and never gives in to production's pressure to 'do it cheaper' or 'closer to the audience'. Ethical responsibility protects lives and one's own career.

In practice: In a real briefing, a producer asks for 'fireworks very close to the crowd, in the packed stadium'. The professional operator answers neither 'yes' nor 'no' right away: he notes the artistic wish, measures the venue, calculates the safety distance required by the size of the product, checks the legislation, and only then proposes an equivalent safe solution (for example, indoor cold sparks instead of aerial shells). Knowing how to say 'I can do this another way, safely' is the mark of the professional.

Summary

  • The effects operator unites art, science and legal responsibility.
  • Safety is the central axis of the whole craft, not a detail.
  • Synchronization, teamwork and ethics define the complete professional.

Art, Science and Responsibility: the pillars of the effects operator

The live special effects operator is not just a technician — he is a professional who unites artistic sensibility (timing, aesthetics, reading the stage), scientific mastery (calculation of distances, pressure, fluids, equipment failures) and ethical responsibility (audience safety, protocols, communication). These three pillars are inseparable: the absence of any one of them compromises the event and can put lives at risk. The simulator presents real field situations — from pre-event to the live stage — where the student experiences how each decision reflects their balance between art, technique and responsibility, developing the judgment that only conscious practice builds.

bf5c88f3-3788-4778-82d0-077255645a2c.titulo: Types of effect and categories: from the aerial shell to cold sparks
bf5c88f3-3788-4778-82d0-077255645a2c.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Knowing the vocabulary and the family of effects is the first step to designing with safety and creativity.

There is an enormous variety of effects, each with its own behavior, risk and application. This lesson organizes the major categories — aerial, ground, water, indoor stage effects and non-pyrotechnic effects (CO2, smoke, confetti) — and explains what each one does, how it works and where it is safe to use. Mastering this taxonomy avoids the serious mistake of using an outdoor product in an enclosed space, for example.

  • Aerial fireworks — shell (bomb): A device launched by a mortar that explodes at altitude, releasing stars. The projection/propulsion charge propels the shell; a delay burns during the ascent; the burst charge ruptures it at the apex, spreading the pellets (stars) that produce the effect. It requires large safety distances and is typically OUTDOOR.
  • Classic shell patterns: Chrysanthemum (sphere of stars with a tail, resembling a chrysanthemum), Peony (sphere without a tail), Willow (stars descending like willow branches), Brocade Crown (shimmering crown), Crossette (stars that split into a cross), Coco/Coconut and Strobe (flashing stars). Each pattern is defined by the internal arrangement of the stars and by the charge.
  • Comet: A single star or effect that travels a visible trajectory, leaving a trail. It can be aerial or aquatic. It creates lines and movements in space.
  • Ground fireworks: Cascade: sparks that fall continuously, creating a 'curtain' of fire. Gerb (gerb/fount): a vertical column of sparks/flame from the ground. Fountain: a controlled jet of sparks, the basis of many stage effects. Mines: an explosion from the ground that launches stars upward. Spinner/Catherine Wheel: a wheel that rotates emitting light and color.
  • Water fireworks: Water comet and geyser (illuminated jet over water), mounted on floating bases. They require extra care with buoyancy, anchoring and electrical contact with the water.
  • Sound and special effects: Salute/report (loud sound, little visual), crackle (crackling), and special effects such as fireworks that form patterns. The salute is regulated, and the sound pressure level is a factor for hearing safety and nuisance.
  • Indoor stage effects (not necessarily pyrotechnic): Cold sparks (Sparkular and similar): jets of low-temperature sparks, designed for enclosed spaces and controlled proximity — they are not classic pyrotechnics, but they still require distance and care. CO2 jets, smoke/haze machines, low fog (dry ice), confetti and streamer launchers, certified indoor pyrotechnics (category T1, stage). The golden rule: an indoor product is designed and certified for indoor; never swap one for the other.
  • Color categories and why they matter: Colors come from chemistry: strontium salts (red), barium (green), copper (blue), sodium (yellow/gold), magnesium/aluminum (bright white). The operator does not manufacture these compounds, but understands that the color indicates composition and therefore burn behavior and residues.

Equipment / details

  • Sparkular / cold spark machines (spark generator category): They generate jets of sparks at reduced temperature using a granulated metallic powder (typically titanium/alloy-based) that is reloadable. Configurable heights (e.g., ~1 to 5 m depending on model). Indoor use and controlled proximity to audience/artists, always respecting the distance indicated by the manufacturer. They safely replace the use of pyrotechnic gerbs in enclosed spaces.
  • Le Maitre (professional indoor pyrotechnics): Manufacturer of certified stage effects — gerbs, comets, mines, flashes, fountains in cartridges for use with firing systems. Categorized products (e.g., T1) for short stage distances. Always use the cartridge in the manufacturer's correct base/holder.
  • Galaxis (pyrotechnic firing system): Professional wireless and wired firing system with addressable modules and time code support, used in large shows. It comes in here as the controller that commands the various types of effect in a synchronized and safe way.

Step by step

  1. 1. Classify the environment: First define whether the effect is indoor (enclosed, low ceiling) or outdoor (open sky). This immediately eliminates entire categories of product.
  2. 2. Choose the effect family: Aerial, ground, water or stage — according to the desired artistic result and the available space.
  3. 3. Check the product's category/certification: Confirm on the label the category (T1, T2... or equivalent), the safety distance and whether it is approved for the chosen environment.
  4. 4. List behavior and risks: For each effect note: height, debris projection, heat, smoke, noise and residue. This data feeds the distance calculation and the risk analysis.

In practice: They asked for a 'cascade of fire coming down from the ceiling' in an enclosed music venue. A traditional pyrotechnic cascade is OUTDOOR and generates heat and residue incompatible with a low ceiling. The professional replaces it with cold sparks (Sparkular) mounted inverted or with a certified Le Maitre indoor fountain, keeping the manufacturer's distance to curtains, grid and audience. Same visual effect, controlled risk.

Summary

  • Effects are divided into aerial, ground, water and indoor stage.
  • Each category has its own behavior, risk and environment — they are not freely interchangeable.
  • Cold sparks and CO2 are safe alternatives for proximity and enclosed spaces.

Categories of Pyrotechnic and Stage Effects

Scenic effects are classified from F1 (low risk, indoor use) to F4 (high power, exclusively outdoor use), according to the ABNT NBR 9820 standard and NR-19. Each category requires specific safety distances and qualifications: cold sparks and CO₂ operate a few meters from the audience, whereas aerial shells demand clearances of up to 200 meters and a licensed operator. Beyond pyrotechnic effects, physical resources such as CO₂ jets and confetti cannons have their own risks — oxygen displacement and extreme temperature — and require a safety protocol even without combustion. Mastery of these categories allows the events professional to specify the correct effect for each environment, scale and audience, ensuring a spectacle with safety.

9ca798bd-6672-4d30-a61d-36d680b0d994.titulo: How a pyrotechnic device works: composition, charges and ignition
9ca798bd-6672-4d30-a61d-36d680b0d994.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Understanding what happens inside the tube is what separates the safe operator from the amateur.

To operate safely you need to understand the anatomy of an effect: the pyrotechnic composition, the different charges (projection, burst, effect), the initiator and the delay. This lesson explains these elements based on the Brazilian technical standard, without going into manufacturing — the events operator buys an approved product, they do not produce gunpowder.

  • Pyrotechnic composition: A mixture of an oxidizer (which provides oxygen) and a fuel, capable of producing effects of projection, propulsion, sound, light, smoke or combinations. Examples from the standard: black powder, white powder, smoke mixes, delay mixes, ignition mixes, effect charges. The operator does not handle these mixtures in bulk; they deal with the finished, sealed product.
  • The charges of a shell: Projection/propulsion charge: propels the shell out of the mortar (typically black powder). Burst charge: ruptures the shell at the apex and spreads the pellets/stars. Effect charge: responsible for the final effect (color, brightness, crackle). Understanding this sequence explains why there is a delay between the firing and the effect in the sky.
  • Initiator and ignition: The initiator (igniter/fuse) is the device that, by flame, friction, percussion or electric current, provides the initial heat. In the professional world the electric initiator (e-match) is used: the firing panel sends current that heats a filament and ignites the product. The standard identifies the common initiator by its dark brown coloring and the electric one by its orange or dark brown color, protected against accidental firing by static electricity.
  • Pyrotechnic delay: A slow-burning device that creates a waiting time between the ignition and the burst/effect charge, compatible with safety and with the desired effect. It is what makes the shell explode at the right height, and not on the ground. The operator needs to know this time in order to synchronize.
  • Launch tube (mortar): A muzzle-loading tube, of paper, high-density plastic or fiberglass, dimensioned to withstand the service pressure. Fiberglass is the modern professional standard for its strength. One never uses an improvised, damaged or prohibited-material tube.
  • What the operator does NOT do: Does not alter compositions, does not open products, does not patch fuses, does not improvise charges. The Brazilian standard prohibits dangerous substances in fireworks (arsenic, chlorates outside exceptions, lead, mercury, picric acid, etc.). Trusting the approved and intact product is part of safety.

Equipment / details

  • Electric initiator / e-match: Pyrotechnic head with two wires; the panel sends current and it ignites the product. Standard parameters: maximum NON-ignition current 0.300 A for at least 5 min; minimum ignition current 0.600 A for 10 ms; cables of at least 5 m. Handled with the system in safe mode (de-energized) until the moment of firing.
  • Fiberglass mortar (launch tube): Professional tube for shells, reinforced with epoxy (minimum strength per standard). Safer than paper/plastic against rupture. Used in racks fixed to the ground, angled away from the audience.
  • Approved pyrotechnic product (shell, cake, gerb, fountain): Cake: a block with several tubes that fire effects in rapid sequence from a single cue — widely used for concentrating effect with one firing. Always acquired with an invoice, within validity, with a legible label and a compatible category.

Step by step

  1. 1. Read the label: Confirm nomenclature, category/class, technical head (CRQ), instructions for use, permitted environment, safe distance and number of shots (in the case of cakes).
  2. 2. Inspect the integrity: The product must be intact, clean, without tears, cracks, loose parts or moisture. A damaged product is discarded according to the supplier's/authority's guidance — never used.
  3. 3. Identify the initiator: Check the color and protection of the initiator. Keep the system de-energized until firing. Operators do not carry a cell phone during ignition (standard).
  4. 4. Position in the correct tube/holder: Each product in its mortar or manufacturer's base, with the clearance and the delay exposed as per the standard (e.g., the initiator's delay exposed at least 150 mm outside the tube after inserting the shell).

In practice: On receiving a box of shells, the operator checks the invoice, seal, label and validity; sets aside a visibly dented unit and returns it to the supplier instead of using it. He loads each shell into the fiberglass mortar matching the caliber, connects the e-match with the system de-energized, and only arms the panel at the agreed moment, with no cell phone in his pocket.

Summary

  • The effect results from projection, burst and effect charges plus a delay.
  • The operator uses an approved and intact product — never manufactures or alters.
  • Electric initiator and the correct mortar, with a safe system until firing.

How a pyrotechnic device works

A pyrotechnic device is composed of multiple functional layers: the propellant projects the shell through the launch tube, a delay column burns during the ascending trajectory and, at the highest point, the burst charge opens the casing and ignites the stars — small pellets with metallic salts that produce the colors and visual effects. Electric ignition transmits current from the firing panel to the e-match (bridgewire), which heats up and starts the burning of the propellant. Safety depends on the correct calculation of circuit resistance, operator distance and weather conditions — all regulated by the Brazilian Army and the Fire Department.

792a9e3e-1ff0-4298-8b3e-8662550459c8.titulo: Delay, synchronization and reading time in the show
792a9e3e-1ff0-4298-8b3e-8662550459c8.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The right effect at the wrong time becomes an error. Timing is safety and it is art.

Delay is the interval between triggering an effect and it actually happening. Every product has its own, and ignoring it destroys synchronization and can create risk. This lesson teaches you to think in terms of time: the physical delay of the product (ascent and delay) and the system delay (electronics, video, network), and how to compensate for them in planning.

  • What pyrotechnic delay is: It is the lag between the ignition and the visual/audible effect. In a shell, the time of ascent through the air is added to the internal delay. A larger-caliber shell rises higher and takes longer — the cue must be anticipated so that the effect 'hits' at the desired musical moment.
  • Product delay vs. system delay: Product delay: physical, of the device itself (ascent + delay). System delay: technological — panel processing, network latency in wireless/IP systems, video cable lag, audio-video desync on panels. Both add up and must be measured, not guessed.
  • Strategic use of delay: Programming different delays across multiple devices creates patterns, waves and sequences (barrage). It is how the operator 'writes' the show in time. But every programmed delay must respect safety margins — never shorten the time in a way that makes effects dangerously overlap.
  • Why wireless and Wi-Fi systems require attention: Wi-Fi connections and congested networks can introduce variable latency — bad for synchronization and dangerous if unpredictable. That is why the professional standard prioritizes dedicated radio frequency or cable, with systems designed for firing (Galaxis, FireOne, etc.), and not generic networks.
  • Compensating for delay in the project: In the script, each cue is given an 'anticipation time' calculated from the known delay of the product and the system. Simul-firing (rehearsal/firing simulation) serves precisely to measure and adjust these times before the real show.

Equipment / details

  • Pyrotechnic sequencer / controller: An electronic device that controls the sequence and timing of firings (cues), allowing delays to be programmed per channel. It is the heart of synchronization. Professional models accept time code to align with audio/video.
  • Time code generator/reader (SMPTE / MTC): Equipment that distributes the common time reference among sound, light, video and pyrotechnics. It allows delay to be compensated consistently across all systems.

Step by step

  1. 1. Determine the delay of each product: Consult manufacturer/data sheet: ascent time + delay. For stage effects, the delay is usually small; for shells, seconds.
  2. 2. Measure the system delay: In rehearsal, fire a test e-match and time the panel's response; assess network latency if wireless.
  3. 3. Calculate the cue anticipation: Anticipate the firing in the script by the total (product + system) so the effect coincides with the musical point.
  4. 4. Validate in simul-firing: Run the sequence in a test (without real effect or in a safe environment) and adjust until the synchronization is correct and safe.

In practice: For a shell to burst exactly on the beat of the chorus, the operator discovers that it takes ~2.5 s between the cue and the explosion (ascent + delay) and that the panel adds ~0.1 s. He programs the cue 2.6 s before the beat. In the rehearsal with time code, he confirms and refines. Without this calculation, the effect would visibly 'lag'.

Summary

  • Delay is the lag between triggering and the effect happening; it always exists.
  • Product delay (ascent + delay) and system delay add up.
  • Anticipating cues and rehearsing in simul-firing ensures safe synchronization.

Delay, Synchronization and Reading Time in the Show

At events with multiple speaker zones (main PA, mid and rear-of-audience delays), each speaker needs to reproduce the signal with a calculated delay so that the sound arrives simultaneously at the listener at each point. The base formula is delay (ms) = (d_zone – d_ref) / v × 1000, where v = 331.3 + 0.606 × T°C m/s. Without this alignment, the listener perceives echo or phase coloration (comb-filter). The Haas effect (1–40 ms of extra delay) ensures that the main PA “arrives first” psychoacoustically, preserving the illusion that the sound comes from the stage even with delay speakers close to the audience.

c78dd78e-0088-4a4f-a133-62ae2cd49833.titulo: CO2 jets and smoke/haze machines: atmospheric effect with safety
c78dd78e-0088-4a4f-a133-62ae2cd49833.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Cloud, jet and haze turn the air into scenery — but pressurized gas and enclosed spaces demand respect.

Atmospheric effects are ubiquitous in shows: CO2 jets that burst white clouds, smoke and haze machines that create atmosphere and reveal light beams, and low fog (dry ice) that covers the floor. This lesson covers the real equipment, how to set it up and operate it, and the specific risks — pressure, asphyxiation in a confined space and low temperature.

  • CO2 jets (cryo jets): They use pressurized liquid CO2 in a cylinder; when released, the gas expands and forms a cold white jet that is visually striking. Triggered manually or via DMX/control system, synchronized with sound and light. CO2 is cold enough to cause frostbite and, in large volume in an enclosed space, to displace oxygen.
  • Components of the CO2 system: Pressurized cylinder (with siphon/dip tube to draw liquid CO2), high-pressure hoses with connectors and sealing rings, valve/solenoid valve, and the ejection nozzle (jet) fixed to truss or base. Firm fixing with clamps and metal straps is mandatory — a loose hose under pressure is a dangerous whip.
  • Smoke machines vs. haze: Smoke machine (fogger): produces a dense, visible cloud, great for impact, dissipates relatively quickly. Haze machine (hazer): produces a fine, homogeneous mist that stays suspended, ideal for revealing light and laser beams without 'polluting' the stage. Both use a heated glycol/glycerin-based fluid.
  • Low fog / dry ice: Low smoke that stays close to the floor (the 'cloud on the floor' effect). Obtained with dry ice (solid CO2 at -78.5 °C) in hot water, or with low fog machines with cooling. Risks: frostbite when handling dry ice (always with thermal gloves) and low CO2 accumulation, which can asphyxiate in pits, wells or unventilated areas.
  • Ventilation and detection: In enclosed spaces, CO2 and dense smoke require ventilation. CO2 is heavier than air and accumulates at the bottom; in large volumes it is recommended to monitor and ensure air renewal. Fire detection systems based on smoke detection can trigger falsely with fog/haze — align with the brigade/firefighters beforehand.
  • Comfort and health: Excess glycol smoke irritates the respiratory tracts of sensitive artists and audience members; use the smallest amount that delivers the effect. Warn artists about nearby CO2 jets (cold and pressure noise).

Equipment / details

  • MagicFX (CO2 jets and systems): A reference manufacturer for CO2 effects (Stadium Shot, CO2 Jet, Swirl Fan). Systems with a solenoid valve controlled by DMX, connected to a liquid CO2 cylinder with a siphon tube. Specs vary by model (typical CO2 working pressure ~50–60 bar). Used for vertical/lateral jets and CO2 curtains on stages and arenas.
  • Antari (smoke, haze and low fog machines): A broad line: fog machines (e.g., Z series), water/oil-based hazers and low fog (e.g., ICE/IP series that cool the smoke to keep it low). Good value for money, widely used at events. Select the fluid recommended by the manufacturer.
  • MDG (high-end haze and fog): A premium manufacturer (ATMe, theONE) known for ultra-fine and consistent haze, with low fluid consumption — a standard in TV, theater and large tours where one wants to reveal light without a heavy visible cloud.
  • Look Solutions (Unique, Viper, Tiny): A German manufacturer of hazers (Unique) and fog machines (Viper) widely used in professional productions; the Unique generates haze by friction/heating with its own fluid, excellent for light beams.
  • CO2 cylinder with siphon tube (dip tube): For jets, LIQUID CO2 is needed; the cylinder must have an internal siphon tube (or be used inverted depending on type) so that liquid comes out and not just gas. The cylinder is always chained/fixed upright, with valve protection.

Step by step

  1. 1. Position and fix the jets: Mount the CO2 nozzles at strategic points (sides/front of stage), away from flammable materials. Fix to truss with steel clamps and secure with metal straps — never merely resting.
  2. 2. Connect high-pressure hoses: Use specific connectors with intact sealing rings. Verify that each connection is firm. Secure the hoses along the structure with zip ties to prevent tripping and whipping.
  3. 3. Fix and open the cylinder carefully: Cylinder upright, chained. Open the valve slowly. Confirm the absence of leakage (escaping sound, ice on the connection indicates a leak).
  4. 4. Test the jet and adjust: With the area cleared, do a short test firing, adjust direction and height. Confirm that no one is in the line of the jet at close range (frostbite/pressure risk).
  5. 5. Position smoke/haze and check detection: Position foggers/hazers for the desired coverage; confirm with the brigade that the venue's fire detection system will not trigger a false alarm; ensure ventilation.
  6. 6. Close and depressurize during teardown: Close the cylinder, relieve the residual pressure in the hoses before disconnecting, and only then dismantle. Handle dry ice only with thermal gloves.

In practice: At an indoor show the operator installs two MagicFX CO2 jets at the front of the stage and an MDG hazer at the back. He chains the cylinder with the siphon tube, tests each connection with soapy water to detect leaks, does a test burst with the area clear, aligns with the brigade to lock out the smoke alarm during the cues, and keeps a ventilation door open. No dry ice in an unventilated pit.

Summary

  • CO2 jets use pressurized liquid gas — risk of cold, pressure and asphyxiation.
  • Smoke (dense) and haze (fine) create atmosphere; low fog covers the floor.
  • Firm fixing, ventilation and alignment with the brigade are mandatory.

CO₂ and Smoke/Haze Machines: Atmospheric Effect with Safety

CO₂ jets create icy visual explosions of short duration, ideal for musical peaks; because they are heavier than air, the gas accumulates at the bottom and can reduce the O₂ on stage — requiring a minimum distance from the audience, active ventilation and continuous monitoring. Haze machines, in turn, produce ultra-fine particles in suspension that enhance lasers and spotlights, while dense smoke machines generate substantial columns with a floor-level scenic effect. In both cases, the base fluid must be certified (water-based), the smoke detectors must be managed with the fire department/responsible person, and the operator must know the evacuation protocols before starting any effect.

c2ab24f1-e671-4bee-b3ea-facd07d5d57c.titulo: Confetti, streamers, cold sparks and controlled fire
c2ab24f1-e671-4bee-b3ea-facd07d5d57c.conteudo_rico:
To begin: From confetti that fills the air to controlled flame: effects of great impact and variable risk.

This lesson brings together effects widely used at show finales and climax moments: confetti and streamer launchers (colorful impact with low risk), cold sparks (high impact, low but real thermal risk) and controlled fire/flames (high thermal risk). We will look at real equipment and how to operate each one while respecting distances and the surroundings.

  • Confetti and streamer launchers: They fire confetti or streamers by CO2/compressed air (cannons) or by a continuous system. They create a festive visual impact with low fire risk, but generate residue (cleanup) and can be slippery on the floor. Confetti must be flame-retardant and, in some venues, biodegradable due to environmental requirements.
  • Types of launcher: Handheld (manual, disposable or CO2-reloadable), stage cannon (fixed, fed by a CO2/air cylinder), and continuous confetti blower (a fan that throws confetti continuously, for long showers). Positioning and direction define the coverage.
  • Cold sparks revisited (operation): Sparkular-type machines generate jets of sparks at a temperature much lower than traditional pyrotechnics, using reloadable granulated metallic powder, controlled by DMX. They are 'cold' enough for indoor use and proximity, BUT they are not entirely risk-free: there are still sparks and localized heat — respecting the manufacturer's distance from people, fabrics, curtains and grid is mandatory.
  • Controlled fire / flame projectors: Flame effects use gas (LPG) and controlled ignition to launch balls/columns of fire. They are HIGH thermal risk, normally outdoor or on stages with a generous ceiling height and clearance, and almost always require specific approval from the brigade/firefighters. Wind and a low ceiling are prohibitive.
  • Residue, cleanup and the environment: Every effect generates fallout (residue): confetti, cartridge remains, spark powder, soot. The operator plans the cleanup, separates residues and respects environmental requirements (biodegradable confetti, debris collection). A floor with confetti becomes a fall risk — signal/clean it.

Equipment / details

  • MagicFX (Confetti/Streamer cannons and Power Shot): Confetti and streamer launchers powered by CO2, with configurable range and DMX control. They allow synchronized firing in arenas and stages. Use flame-retardant confetti refills recommended by the manufacturer.
  • Sparkular (cold sparks): A cold spark machine with granulated powder refill, adjustable height (models with ~up to 5 m) and DMX control; several units synchronize a 'curtain' of sparks. Safety distance and orientation per the manual; keep away from smoke detectors and flammable materials.
  • Le Maitre / Galaxis (flame effects and flame projectors): Gas-fueled controlled fire systems with ignition and valve commanded by a safe controller; they require a certified operator, ample clearance and firefighter approval. Never improvise fire with gas.
  • Handheld CO2 confetti launcher: A reloadable manual cannon; handy for one-off moments. Always direct it upward/away from eyes and never at people at close range.

Step by step

  1. 1. Define the moment and coverage: Confetti/streamers are usually for the climax/finale. Calculate the number and position of launchers to cover the desired area without aiming directly at the audience at close range.
  2. 2. Load the correct refills: Use only flame-retardant refills compatible with the launcher. Connect to CO2/air as per the manufacturer.
  3. 3. Position cold sparks with clearance: Mount the Sparkular machines with the manual's safety distance to people, curtains, grid and sensors. Set height and angle. Do a short test.
  4. 4. Treat controlled fire as a special category: For flames, validate with firefighters, ensure clearance and the absence of wind/low ceiling, and use only a certified operator and system.
  5. 5. Plan the cleanup and the residue: Reserve crew and time to collect confetti and debris; signal a slippery floor; separate residues according to environmental requirements.

In practice: At the finale of an indoor show, the operator programs four MagicFX confetti cannons on the sides (aimed upward, flame-retardant refill) and six Sparkular at the stage front, all via DMX on the same cue. He measures the distance from the Sparkular to the curtain and the audience, does a short test, confirms with the brigade and keeps a cleanup crew ready to collect the confetti right after.

Summary

  • Confetti and streamers have low fire risk, but generate residue and a slippery floor.
  • Cold sparks allow indoor proximity, but require the manufacturer's distance.
  • Controlled fire is high risk: only with firefighter approval and ample clearance.

Special Stage Effects: Sparks, Confetti and Controlled Fire

Confetti, streamers, cold sparks and controlled flames are effects of great visual impact widely used at events. Each one requires specific equipment, correct configuration of pressure and charge, and a rigorous safety protocol — including an isolated perimeter, adequate PPE, a positioned extinguisher and communication with the operations director. Cold sparks operate by oxidation of metallic powder at low temperature, without a real flame, but still require distance from the audience. Controlled fire, in turn, demands technical qualification, a Fire Department license and gas purging before and after firing. In this simulator, the student practices the operational sequence of each effect — configuring parameters, completing the checklist and monitoring the equipment readings — before facing the pressure of a live show.

7b289dc0-2f7c-4869-a482-8c9d91654a1e.titulo: Firing panel: anatomy, setup and operation
7b289dc0-2f7c-4869-a482-8c9d91654a1e.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The panel is the brain of the show. Knowing how to set it up and operate it safely is a core competency of the operator.

The firing panel (console or pyrotechnic controller) is the equipment that arms, organizes and fires the effects in a coordinated and safe way. This lesson is a practical manual: what makes up the panel, how to set it up, how to test continuity, how to program and how to operate in manual mode — always with locks and emergency procedures.

  • The control console: The central part where the operator interacts: buttons, switches, screens (some touch-sensitive), channel selectors and the ARM button/key and FIRE. It is where sequences are programmed and the system status is monitored.
  • Addressable modules and channels: Firing modules (slats/cue boxes) connect to the console and expand capacity; each module controls a number of channels, and each channel triggers one or more e-matches. In modern systems the modules are addressable and can be distributed around the stage, connected by cable or radio.
  • Electrical connections and power: The panel connects to the products via firing cables; a power source (battery/UPS) keeps the system operational. Firm, identified connections are critical for firing reliability.
  • The panel's safety system: Professional panels have an arming key (key switch), a safety lock, status indicators, fault/continuity detection and an emergency stop (abort). The system is only energized for firing when explicitly armed, and there is always a way to abort.
  • Continuity test: Before the show, the continuity of each circuit is tested: the panel verifies (with a very low current, below the ignition current) whether the e-match and the cables are connected and intact, without firing. An 'open' circuit indicates a bad connection or a defective e-match — it is corrected before the event.
  • Manual vs. programmed operation: In manual mode, the operator fires each cue live, on time (following the music and cues via headphones). In programmed/time code mode, the sequence runs automatically aligned to the time reference. Even on automatic, the operator keeps a finger on the abort.
  • Indicators and communication: Lights and audible signals report status (armed, continuity ok, fault). The operator monitors everything and maintains communication via headphones/radio with the show's direction.

Equipment / details

  • Galaxis Pyrotechnics (Pro G-FLAME / GFC Advanced): A professional wired and wireless system, addressable modules, high reliability and time code support; used in large shows and arenas. It performs a continuity test per channel and offers robust safety locks.
  • FireOne / Pyrodigital (pyrotechnic controllers): Established digital firing systems, widely used in large spectacles synchronized with music and SMPTE time code; modules with many cues and dedicated programming software.
  • Galaxis / Cobra / Le Maitre wireless (addressable modules): Wireless firing modules that receive cues from the console via dedicated radio (not Wi-Fi), allowing firing points to be distributed around the stage with reliable synchronization and remote continuity testing.
  • Power source/battery and identified firing cables: Dedicated power (sealed battery/UPS) and cables labeled per circuit. Labeling each cable prevents mismatches between channel and effect.

Step by step

  1. 1. Position the panel: Install the console in a safe location, away from the stage and the audience, with a clear view of the show and, preferably, at an elevated point. Ensure a clear path to abort/evacuate.
  2. 2. Connect modules and power: Connect the modules to the console (cable or radio pairing), connect the power source/battery and confirm everything is in SAFE mode (disarmed), with no power on the channels.
  3. 3. Connect and identify circuits: Connect the products' e-matches to the channels; label each circuit indicating which effect it triggers. Organize the cables to prevent tripping and damage.
  4. 4. Test continuity: With the system still disarmed for firing, run the continuity test channel by channel. Correct any open/faulty circuit before proceeding. Do NOT arm while there are people in the firing zone.
  5. 5. Program or prepare the manual: Load the cue sequence (with delays) for time code, or organize the cue sheet for manual operation. Do a simul-firing if possible.
  6. 6. Arm only at the right moment: Confirm the area is isolated and the crew is aware. Use the ARM key only when everything is cleared. Keep a finger on the abort throughout the show.
  7. 7. Disarm and unload during teardown: At the end, disarm, remove the key, disconnect the power and the circuits, and confirm there is no power before handling unfired products.

In practice: The operator sets up the Galaxis console on a technical tower with a view of the stage, connects the modules via dedicated radio, labels each circuit ('CO2 front', 'gerb back L1'...), runs the continuity test and discovers channel 7 is open — he re-tightens the e-match and the test passes. Only after the area is isolated and the crew confirms 'stage clear' does he insert the ARM key, with his thumb over the abort button.

Summary

  • The firing panel arms, organizes and fires the effects with safety locks.
  • The continuity test checks circuits without firing, before arming.
  • The panel is armed only with the area isolated; the operator keeps the abort always accessible.

Firing Panel: Anatomy, Setup and Operation

The firing panel (or DMX console) is the heart of light and effects control at events. It sends commands over the DMX-512 protocol — a serial line with up to 512 channels per universe — to all fixtures connected in a chain (daisy chain). Each fixture receives a unique address configured via dipswitch or display; the operator associates these addresses with the channel in the console's patch. Operation is organized into cues: pre-programmed scenes with levels of intensity, position and color that the operator fires sequentially during the show using the GO button. The master fader controls the global intensity of all outputs, while the channel faders allow individual adjustments per group of fixtures. Special effects such as CO₂, laser and strobes require extra attention to safety before each firing.

5598dcb8-fcc0-4bab-a11d-513b725189af.titulo: Time code and synchronization: SMPTE, MTC and manual operation
5598dcb8-fcc0-4bab-a11d-513b725189af.conteudo_rico:
To begin: When sound, light, video and fire must hit the exact same instant, time code is the common language.

Time code is a shared time reference that keeps all systems synchronized. This lesson explains the formats (SMPTE, MTC), how to integrate pyrotechnics with time code, and when it makes sense to operate manually rather than automatically.

  • What time code is: A timekeeping system (hours:minutes:seconds:frames) that acts as the master clock for every department. Each cue has an address in time; when the time code reaches that point, the effect fires automatically, in sync with audio, light and video.
  • Formats: SMPTE and MTC: SMPTE (LTC/audio): the industry-standard video/audio time code, robust, distributed over an audio cable. MTC (MIDI Time Code): the version over MIDI. The pyrotechnic system must read/receive the format used by the production to align its cues.
  • Master vs slave: One device is the 'master' that generates the time code (usually the audio/video playback) and the rest (light, pyrotechnics) are 'slaves' that follow. Defining who is the master prevents conflicts and ensures everyone starts from the same clock.
  • Integrating pyrotechnics: A pyrotechnic controller with a time code input is used (FireOne, Pyrodigital, Galaxis). In the software, each cue receives a timestamp; when the system receives the time code, it executes the cues at the programmed instant, already compensating for the calculated delays.
  • When to operate manually: In smaller shows, with simple cues, or when there is improvisation/live variation (a band that extends a section), manual operation is safer and more flexible: the operator follows the music and fires on time. Many shows combine automatic blocks (fine sync) with manual cues (variable moments).
  • Risks and precautions of automatic sync: If the time code fails or freezes, cues may fire wrong or all at once — which is why there are safeguards: a lockout if the time code is inconsistent, and the abort always available. Testing the time code chain during rehearsal is mandatory.

Equipment / details

  • Pyrotechnic controller with time code input (FireOne / Pyrodigital / Galaxis): Receives SMPTE/MTC and executes the cue list at the programmed time. Allows delays per cue to be programmed and locks firing if the time code is invalid.
  • SMPTE (LTC) generator/distributor: A playback device or software that generates the master time code and distributes it over an audio cable to light, video and pyrotechnics. The consistency of that signal is the foundation of sync.
  • Show programming software (show creator): The pyrotechnic system's software where you build the timeline, place each cue on the time code and simulate the sequence (virtual simul-firing) before the real firing.

Step by step

  1. 1. Define the time code master: Agree with production which device generates the time code (usually the audio playback) and in what format (SMPTE/MTC).
  2. 2. Connect pyrotechnics as slave: Connect the pyrotechnic controller's time code input to the SMPTE distribution. Confirm a stable code reading.
  3. 3. Program the timeline: In the software, place each cue at the desired instant, already anticipating the product and system delays.
  4. 4. Rehearse the complete chain: Run the time code end to end in rehearsal (simul-firing) to validate that pyrotechnics, light and video hit together. Check the behavior if the code freezes.
  5. 5. Define manual blocks: Mark the moments that will be fired manually (variable live) and rehearse the transition between automatic and manual.

In practice: For a show with a live band, the operator runs the choreographed blocks (intro and finale) via SMPTE coming from the playback, but keeps the middle firings on manual, because the band tends to improvise. In rehearsal, he simulates a time code failure and confirms that the system locks out automatic firings instead of releasing everything at once — and that the abort responds.

Summary

  • Time code is the common clock that synchronizes sound, light, video and pyrotechnics.
  • SMPTE/MTC with one master and the rest as slaves ensures alignment.
  • Manual operation is safer in variable moments; the abort is always kept.

Timecode: the event's time ruler

SMPTE LTC (Linear Time Code) is an encoded audio signal that carries hours, minutes, seconds and frames — recorded on tape or distributed over cable to synchronize multiple pieces of equipment simultaneously. MTC (MIDI Time Code) translates that same information into MIDI quarter-frame messages, allowing lighting consoles, media servers and electronic instruments to be synchronized over the MIDI network. The frame rate (23.976, 24, 25, 29.97 DF or 30 fps) must be identical across every device in the chain; any mismatch produces drift that, in special effects and laser systems, represents a real safety risk. When the timecode system fails, manual operation requires physical cue lists, a stopwatch and precise verbal communication among the technical crew.

1a80aaee-b376-4385-bf5f-956314dc0fc0.titulo: Panels and non-pyrotechnic visual effects: LED, pixel and stage fire
1a80aaee-b376-4385-bf5f-956314dc0fc0.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Not all impact comes from fire: panels and light are part of the complete effects operator's arsenal.

LED panels, pixel mapping, strobes and other visual resources complement (and sometimes safely replace) pyrotechnics. This lesson presents the types of panel, how they are operated and the problem of video delay/audio-image sync, essential for integrating visuals and sound.

  • LED video walls and LED panels: High-resolution video walls (LED modules) display images, videos and effects; LED panels create dynamic patterns and backgrounds. They are set up by connecting modules, setting the resolution and controlling them via software/media console. They can simulate 'explosions' and light with no pyrotechnic risk.
  • Pixel mapping: The physical arrangement of the panels/LED points is digitally mapped to control each pixel individually, creating precise patterns and movements synchronized to the show — widely used for effects coordinated with the music.
  • Strobes, LED tubes and LED floor: Strobes produce intense synchronized flashes (beware of the audience's photosensitivity — warnings and limits). LED tubes create outlines and 3D shapes. LED floors interact with performers and audience. All are controlled by a lighting console/DMX or pixel.
  • Fire and water panels with projection: Fire panels use controlled-flame devices (high risk — see the controlled-fire lesson). Water screens/curtains serve as a projection surface. They are combined with mapped video for great impact.
  • Video delay and A/V sync: Panels and video systems introduce latency: internal processing, cables (long HDMI/DisplayPort), congested IP networks, post-processing. If the image lags behind the audio, the sync breaks. This delay is measured and compensated, and quality cabling/networking is used. In IP-controlled systems, network congestion is avoided.
  • When to use visuals instead of pyrotechnics: In venues where fire is prohibited or risky (low ceiling, audience too close, fire-department restriction), LED panels and light deliver impact with much lower risk. The complete operator knows how to propose this swap.

Equipment / details

  • Modular LED panel (video wall): Modules with variable pitch (e.g., P2 to P5 for stage) mounted on a structure; controlled by a video processor and media console. For 'explosion' effects and bright light without pyrotechnics.
  • Pixel controller / video processor: Handles the mapping and synchronization of the pixels with the show timeline, often integrated via DMX/Art-Net and even time code.
  • Professional strobes (DMX): Produce high-intensity flashes synchronized to the music; use with caution due to photosensitivity, with warnings to the audience when intense.

Step by step

  1. 1. Assemble and configure the panel: Connect the modules, set resolution and mapping; power up and test the image.
  2. 2. Integrate into the show control: Link to the video/pixel processor and synchronize with the timeline (DMX/Art-Net/time code as applicable).
  3. 3. Measure and compensate the video delay: Compare image and audio; adjust delays to align A/V; use quality cabling/networking and avoid IP congestion.
  4. 4. Assess safe replacement of pyrotechnics: Where fire is risky, design equivalent light/LED effects to deliver impact with lower risk.

In practice: In a theater with a low ceiling and a fire ban, the operator replaces the pyrotechnic 'explosion' of the climax with a pixel-mapping effect on LED with a synchronized strobe, measuring and compensating the video delay so the image hits exactly on the beat of the sound. A high-impact result, zero fire risk.

Summary

  • LED panels, pixel mapping and strobes complement or replace pyrotechnics.
  • Video delay and A/V sync must be measured and compensated.
  • Where fire is risky, visuals deliver impact with much lower risk.

LED Panels, Pixel Pitch and Stage Fire Effects

The LED panel is today the main visual element of events: its quality depends on the pixel pitch (distance between pixels in mm), which determines the minimum distance for the viewer not to see the individual dots. The rule of thumb is simple — pixel pitch in mm equals the ideal minimum distance in meters (P4 → 4 m). Brightness in nits should be proportional to the ambient lighting: interiors require at least 800 nits, venues with natural light may demand 5000 nits or more. Non-pyrotechnic stage fire (CO₂- or propane-based with a controlled burner), in turn, is a visual effect approved for closed stages, provided the safety distances are respected — the flame must have a minimum clearance of 1 m from ceiling structures and the audience must be at least 2.5× the flame height away.

ef1cd2c1-f67d-4c28-b32e-fce731798bbc.titulo: Safety distances and positioning of effects
ef1cd2c1-f67d-4c28-b32e-fce731798bbc.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The correct distance is the first barrier against accidents. Getting it wrong here means putting lives at risk.

Safety distance is the minimum separation between the firing area and the audience/crew/structures, and it depends on the type and size of the effect. This lesson teaches the practical international rule for shells, the positioning and tilt of the mortars, and the definition of the event's zones. Note: distances vary by legislation and manufacturer — always adopt the LARGEST required value and be conservative.

  • Why distance matters: It keeps audience and crew out of reach of debris, heat, projection and of a possible premature explosion or falling live shell. It is the safeguard that works even when something goes wrong.
  • Rule of thumb for aerial shells: Common international rule: a minimum distance of approximately 70 to 100 feet per inch of shell diameter. Typical examples: 3 in → ~70–90 m; 4 in → ~100–120 m; 6 in → ~150–180 m; 8 in → ~200–240 m. These are reference values: local legislation and the manufacturer may require more — always adopt the largest.
  • Distances for stage and indoor effects: Cold sparks, gerbs and stage fountains have much smaller distances, defined by the manufacturer (to people, curtains, grid and sensors). For CO2 and controlled fire, follow the manual and the fire brigade. Never assume — read the product's spec sheet.
  • The three zones: Firing area (where the effects are), technical area (crew and console) and audience area. The safety distance must be respected among all of them. The firing zone (blast zone) is isolated and access-restricted.
  • Tilt and direction of the mortars: Tubes always tilted AWAY from the audience and structures, in the safe fallout direction of debris. The wind is taken into account: the tilt is set so wind and debris do not carry material over the audience. Racks firmly anchored to the ground.
  • Wind direction and fallout: The fallout zone (where residue and debris land) must be clear of audience and flammable materials, and downwind relative to the audience (wind pushing debris away from people). Reassess if the wind changes.
  • Margin and conservatism: When in doubt, increase the distance. It is better to have a slightly smaller or more distant effect than an accident. The distance is never 'negotiable' under production pressure.

Equipment / details

  • Fiberglass mortar racks and bases: Structures that hold the tubes firmly, allowing a controlled tilt away from the audience. Anchored to the ground (stakes, weight) so they do not tip over on firing.
  • Tape measure/laser meter and site map: To measure and mark the real distances between firing, technical and audience areas on the ground, instead of estimating by eye.
  • Anemometer (wind meter): Measures the wind speed on site; essential for deciding the tilt, fallout position and possible cancellation (wind of ~30 km/h is usually the limit to cancel).

Step by step

  1. 1. Identify the largest effect and its caliber: Determine the largest shell/effect of the show; it defines the largest required distance.
  2. 2. Calculate the minimum distance: Apply the rule (≈70–100 feet per inch) and compare with the legal/manufacturer requirement; adopt the LARGEST value.
  3. 3. Mark the three zones on site: Measure and demarcate with barriers the firing area, the technical area and the audience area, respecting the distance in all directions.
  4. 4. Position and tilt the mortars: Anchor the racks, tilt away from the audience and in the safe fallout direction, taking into account the measured wind.
  5. 5. Reassess with the actual wind: Measure the wind before and during; if it changes direction/intensity, reposition or cancel as appropriate.

In practice: With 4-inch shells, the operator calculates a ~100–120 m safety radius, measures it with a laser tape, demarcates the blast zone with barriers and positions the racks tilted to the side opposite the audience, where there is open field for the fallout. He measures the wind (18 km/h, blowing away from the crowd) and proceeds; he agrees with the crew that if the wind turns toward the audience, the show is reassessed.

Summary

  • Safety distance depends on the size of the effect; always adopt the largest required value.
  • Rule of thumb for shells: ~70–100 feet per inch of diameter.
  • Tilting away from the audience and managing wind/fallout are essential.

Safety Distances and Positioning of Special Effects

Each type of special effect (CO₂, piroflash, laser, smoke, confetti, strobe) has minimum frontal and lateral safety distances that must be respected to protect the audience, the performers and the technical crew. Correct positioning on stage takes into account the effect's reach, the firing angle, the power applied and the signaling and operation conditions. High-risk effects such as piroflash and laser require regulatory authorization, a certified operator and fire-fighting equipment in position. Failure to comply with these distances can result in serious accidents, civil and criminal liability, and cancellation of the event permit.

39e3adaa-3f15-488d-a87e-3ad63982d558.titulo: PPE, collective safety and procedures in case of failure (misfire)
39e3adaa-3f15-488d-a87e-3ad63982d558.conteudo_rico:
To begin: When something goes wrong, it is discipline in the procedures that prevents tragedy.

This lesson covers the professional's protection (PPE), collective protection (extinguishers, barriers, communication) and — crucially — the misfire (firing failure) protocol, the most dangerous moment of a pyrotechnic operation. Here the rule is: patience and procedure, never haste.

  • Mandatory PPE: Safety helmet, protective goggles/visor, ear protection, suitable gloves (flame/heat resistant and, when electrical, antistatic), cotton or flame-resistant antistatic clothing (nothing synthetic that melts), safety boots. The standard also requires full PPE during rehearsals and inspections.
  • Prohibitions during operation: Do not carry matches, lighters, cigarettes or any material that produces sparks; do not use a mobile phone during ignition; never operate under the effect of alcohol, narcotics or medication that affects reflexes and judgment (a requirement of the standard).
  • Collective safety: Suitable extinguishers (class ABC for general use; never water on electrical hazards), emergency lighting, communication radios, physical barriers and signage for restricted areas. Everything positioned and tested before the show.
  • Main risks to anticipate: Misfire (firing failure), premature explosion (in the tube or near the ground), falling live shell, fire at the venue and projection of debris. Frequent causes: assembly error, damaged tube, incorrect ignition, strong wind and electrical failure.
  • Misfire protocol — the most important: If an effect does not fire: DO NOT approach immediately. Wait at least 15 to 30 minutes (the standard sets a minimum of 15 min to examine/remove the failure). Turn off/disarm the ignition system. Only then approach with extreme caution. Isolate the area. Preferably, investigate only after the show ends and the audience has left.
  • What NEVER to do in a misfire: Never try to relight it manually, never immediately remove the device, never tilt/look inside the tube, never rush the waiting time. A shell 'that didn't fire' may fire with a delay and kill anyone nearby.
  • Communication in an emergency: Whoever identifies the failure immediately alerts the chief operator by radio; the decision to wait, isolate or abort is centralized. Stay calm and follow the procedure, no heroes.

Equipment / details

  • Pyrotechnic PPE kit: Helmet, goggles/visor, ear protection, heat-resistant/antistatic gloves, cotton/flame-resistant clothing, safety boots. Checked and in good condition before each operation.
  • Class ABC extinguishers and anti-spark tool: ABC extinguishers positioned near the firing zone; non-sparking tools for handling. Never water on energized circuits.
  • Dedicated communication radios: Clear and immediate communication between crew and chief operator, especially to report failures and coordinate the abort.

Step by step

  1. 1. Put on and check the PPE: Before assembling/operating, everyone puts on the full PPE and checks its condition. Without PPE, no one enters the firing zone.
  2. 2. Position the collective safety: ABC extinguishers, barriers, signage, emergency lighting and radios tested and in position.
  3. 3. In case of misfire — stop: Identified the failure? DO NOT approach. Alert the chief operator by radio.
  4. 4. Wait and disarm: Wait at least 15 to 30 minutes. Turn off/disarm the ignition system. Keep the area isolated.
  5. 5. Approach with caution and neutralize: Only after the waiting time and with the system disarmed, approach with caution to neutralize according to procedure — ideally after the audience leaves.
  6. 6. Record the incident: Document the failure (which circuit, product, probable cause) for the post-event report and for the supplier.

In practice: During the show, the cake on channel 12 does not fire. The assistant alerts by radio: 'misfire channel 12'. The chief operator does NOT send anyone to the spot; he continues the show with the other cues, and at the end isolates the point, disarms the console, waits 30 minutes and only then, with the audience already gone, approaches with caution to neutralize the product. No one tries to relight it or touch the tube before the right time.

Summary

  • Full PPE and a ban on spark sources, mobile phone and substances during operation.
  • Collective safety: ABC extinguishers, barriers, signage and communication.
  • Misfire: wait 15–30 min, disarm, isolate and never relight or touch the tube.

PPE, Collective Safety and Misfire Procedures

In special-effects operations for events, the correct use of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) is a legal obligation under NR-6 and the minimum condition to clear any firing. Each type of effect — CO₂, flamethrower, sparkler, aerial pyro — requires exclusion zones and attention calculated as a function of the charge's power and wind conditions, determining where the audience and crew can be during the operation. In case of a firing failure (misfire), the universal protocol requires an immediate stop, a minimum waiting time of 15 minutes without approaching, and an approach by at most one operator with full PPE. Standardized communication by radio and hand gestures is the final layer of collective safety, ensuring a 'GO' or 'STOP' is received correctly even under intense show noise.

9c77a0bc-aa38-4550-aeec-e5c77534f429.titulo: Weather conditions, storage and emergency plan
9c77a0bc-aa38-4550-aeec-e5c77534f429.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Wind, rain and good storage decide whether the show happens — and whether it happens safely.

This lesson closes the safety module with three pillars: reading the weather (and when to cancel), the safe storage and transport of controlled products, and the emergency plan every show must have. These are decisions that precede the firing and can mean postponing or canceling — always in favor of safety.

  • Influence of the weather: Critical factors: wind speed and direction, rain, humidity and visibility. Strong wind deflects trajectories and carries debris toward the audience; rain and humidity compromise products and the electrical part; low visibility makes visual control harder.
  • Limits and cancellation: It is recommended to cancel/postpone when the wind exceeds about 30 km/h (and there are lower limits for specific effects — by standard, fireworks with parachutes should not be operated with wind above 5 m/s). The standard also prohibits firing fireworks during periods of rain/storm, with or without a forecast of electrical discharges. When in doubt, do not fire.
  • Electrical clearances (standard): Fireworks with electrical ignition must be at least 50 m from high-voltage power lines and 200 m from railways/subway; operators do not carry a mobile phone. These clearances protect against accidental firing by induction/static.
  • Safe storage: A ventilated location, with humidity control, away from heat sources, with controlled access and hazard signage. In Brazil, pyrotechnic products are supervised by the Brazilian Army (controlled products system). Detonators/initiators must be kept separate from the products. The quantity and place of storage follow the authorizations.
  • Transport and handling: Transport in suitable vehicles and packaging, in accordance with the UN classification of explosives and the legislation; handle without impacts; unfired products must be temporarily stored in a safe area and transported according to the standards.
  • Emergency plan: Every show must have: a fire brigade, the presence/contact of an ambulance, communication with the fire department, evacuation routes, a safe meeting point. Mandatory equipment: ABC extinguishers, first-aid kit, flashlights/emergency lighting. Drills and evacuation rehearsals prepare the crew.
  • Continuous monitoring: The weather is monitored before and during the event (anemometer, forecast). The decision to proceed, postpone or abort is dynamic and centralized, and prevails over any commercial pressure.

Equipment / details

  • Anemometer and weather forecast source: Measures wind in real time and tracks the forecast to decide on tilt, fallout and cancellation. Indispensable outdoors.
  • Magazine/container for storing controlled products: An appropriate location, ventilated, dry, signposted and with restricted access, in accordance with Army authorization, with initiators kept separate from the products.
  • Emergency kit (ABC extinguishers, first aid, flashlights): Mandatory equipment of the emergency plan, positioned and tested before the show.

Step by step

  1. 1. Check the weather the day before and on the day: Track the forecast and measure wind on site. Define the cancellation triggers (e.g. wind > 30 km/h, rain/storm).
  2. 2. Verify the site clearances: Confirm the distance from high-voltage lines (≥50 m) and railways/subway (≥200 m) for electrical ignition.
  3. 3. Store and transport according to the standard: Keep products in a controlled location, with initiators separate; transport in suitable packaging/vehicle, without impacts.
  4. 4. Build the emergency plan: Define the brigade, ambulance, contact with the fire department, evacuation routes and meeting point; position extinguishers, first aid and flashlights.
  5. 5. Rehearse the evacuation and monitor: Hold an emergency briefing/drill with the crew; monitor the weather throughout the event and be ready to abort.

In practice: On the day of the outdoor show, the forecast indicates increasing gusts. The operator measures the wind hourly; when it reaches ~32 km/h and changes direction toward the audience, he triggers the plan: he informs production and the fire department and postpones the aerial fireworks, keeping only safe stage effects. The unused products go back to controlled storage, with initiators separate. The decision to postpone prevails over the pressure to 'do it anyway'.

Summary

  • Wind (~30 km/h), rain and storm are reasons for cancellation; when in doubt, do not fire.
  • Products are controlled by the Army: storage and transport follow the standard, with initiators separate.
  • Every show needs an emergency plan, a brigade and continuous weather monitoring.

Weather Conditions, Storage and Emergency Plan

The event's environment is a decisive technical factor for the safety of live special effects. A temperature above 35°C raises the pressure in cylinders and can cause spontaneous deflagration in pyrotechnic devices; relative humidity below 25% increases the risk of a static spark; winds above 30 km/h require suspending low-altitude pyrotechnics and CO₂ jets. Each material — pyrotechnics, CO₂ cylinders, smoke fluids, lasers and generators — has specific storage requirements defined by the standards of the Army, ABNT and occupational safety regulations. The emergency plan must be drawn up before the show, with demarcated evacuation routes, calculated exclusion zones, a briefing with the whole crew and tested communication channels, ensuring a rapid response to any weather or operational incident.

08f5e811-e9a5-45c1-9803-56db1dfc6a09.titulo: Legislation and technical standards for pyrotechnics in Brazil
08f5e811-e9a5-45c1-9803-56db1dfc6a09.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Without documentation and a license, there is no legal show. The legal side is just as technical as the firing itself.

In Brazil, pyrotechnic activity is regulated and overseen mainly by the Brazilian Army (controlled products), with Federal Police rules on registration and control, ABNT technical standards and military regulations. This lesson maps the legal framework faithfully, always advising you to look up the CURRENT versions with the competent bodies, because legislation changes.

  • Attention: legislation changes: The references below come from the base material and may have been updated. ALWAYS confirm the versions in force directly with the Brazilian Army, the Federal Police and state/municipal bodies before any operation. Operating based on a repealed standard is a serious legal risk.
  • Brazilian Army — controlled products: The manufacture, import, storage, transport and use of pyrotechnic products (Class 1 explosives) are overseen by the Army, through the controlled-products regulation (R-105) and technical regulations. Companies and professionals need registration/authorization to operate.
  • Federal Police — registration and control: The material cites Ordinance No. 1,176/2019 of the Federal Police, which deals with the registration of pyrotechnic products, authorization of companies and professionals, technical and safety standards, and procedures for pyrotechnic displays. Check the version in force and any amendments.
  • ABNT standards (NBR 15850 to 15855:2016): A set of standards that classifies and establishes requirements for fireworks: NBR 15855 (classification and general requirements) and NBR 15850 to 15854 (categories T1 to T5). The T categories indicate where/how the product may be used — T1 has the smallest distance (stage/indoor use) and the numbering grows with the risk/distance.
  • Military technical regulation (REG/T-02): Defines requirements for the identification, assembly, composition and operation of fireworks; classifies fireworks in categories A, B, C and D of R-105; establishes prohibited substances, electric-igniter parameters, minimum burst heights, tube thickness and inspection procedures. It is the technical basis for what makes a product 'legal'.
  • Technical officer and CRQ: The packaging must show the name of the technical officer and their registration with the Regional Chemistry Council (CRQ), plus the nomenclature, class, instructions for use, permitted environment, safe distance and number of shots. A product without compliant labeling is a sign of irregularity.
  • State and municipal levels: In addition to the federal level, there are state and municipal rules (fire department, municipal ordinances, noise and time restrictions). It is mandatory to consult local bodies — in many cities there are even rules about fireworks with/without report (bang).

Step by step

  1. 1. Confirm the legislation in force: Before each project, check with the Army, Federal Police and local bodies which standards are in force — do not rely on old versions.
  2. 2. Check the registration/authorization of the company and the professional: Confirm that the company and the professionals have the registration/authorization required to manufacture/sell/use pyrotechnics.
  3. 3. Validate the origin and labeling of the products: Buy from an authorized supplier, with an invoice, and check the label per the standard (nomenclature, class, technical officer/CRQ, distance, environment, number of shots).
  4. 4. Identify local requirements: Consult the fire department and city hall about licenses, noise/time restrictions and specific rules of the municipality/state.

In practice: Before closing a New Year's Eve deal, the operator confirms that the supplier company has Army authorization and that the shells have a label with technical officer/CRQ and category. They call the local fire department and city hall, discover a time restriction for reports (bangs) and adjust the script. They keep a copy of all authorizations in the project folder — without this, there is no show.

Summary

  • The Army (controlled products), the Federal Police and ABNT form the legal basis of pyrotechnics.
  • The T1–T5 categories and labeling with technical officer/CRQ indicate the product's use and legality.
  • Legislation changes and there are state/municipal rules — always confirm what is in force before operating.

Legislation and Technical Standards for Pyrotechnics in Brazil

Professional pyrotechnics in Brazil is regulated by Law 17,716/2023, by Decree 3,665/2000 and by Ordinance SFPC No. 25 of the Brazilian Army, which classify products into 4 categories according to energy released, effect altitude and danger level. Only qualified technicians registered with the SFPC may operate category 3 and 4 products, and any event requires prior authorization at least 30 days in advance. The safety distances — danger, exclusion and audience-setback zones — are calculated based on the mortar diameter and the type of effect, following the guidelines of NBR 15292 and NFPA 1123. Failure to comply with these rules constitutes a crime under the Disarmament Statute (Law 10,826/2003).

3553c87e-188e-4559-91f1-7d8871e78c1d.titulo: Documentation, licenses, technical rider and brigade/fire department
3553c87e-188e-4559-91f1-7d8871e78c1d.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The project folder is your professional life insurance: without paperwork, you don't set foot on stage.

This lesson is the practical side of the legal-administrative: which documents and licenses to gather, how to organize the project documentation, what the technical rider is, and how to coordinate the fire brigade and the fire department. Good documentation protects the audience and legally protects the operator.

  • Essential project documentation: Gather: authorization/registration of the company and professionals (Army/Federal Police), event and fire-department license (AVCB/inspection depending on the venue), invoices and product certificates, insurance policy, technical responsibility record (ART) where applicable, safety plan and emergency plan, and the cue script. All filed and available on the day.
  • Licenses and deadlines: Licenses usually have a request deadline — the fire department and authorities may require lead time (days to weeks). Map the deadlines at the start of the project so as not to jeopardize the event. Operating without a license is illegal and can lead to shutdown and liability.
  • Briefing and script: The briefing captures the client's wishes (moments, music, intensity, visual identity). The script translates this into cues with times and products, the basis for programming the console and for calculating delays and distances.
  • Technical rider: A document that lists everything the effect needs from the venue and the production: power (outlets/panels), fixing points/truss, space for the firing and fallout zone, load-in/load-out access, time code signal, console position, PPE and required crew, and safety requirements. The rider prevents surprises during setup.
  • Crew and roles: Define roles: chief operator (technical officer and abort decision), assembly assistants, safety observers/spotters, liaison with the fire department and production. Each one trained and aware of the plan.
  • Fire brigade and fire department: Arrange the presence of the brigade and/or fire department as required locally; align the use of smoke/fire so as not to trigger alarms; agree on abort signals and an incident-response route. The fire department may veto effects — respect that.
  • Storage and organization of documents: Keep a folder (physical and digital) per event, with a checklist of what was obtained, expiry dates of the licenses and contacts of the authorities. Organized documentation speeds up inspections and protects in case of questioning.
  • Post-event report: After the show, record what happened: cues fired, failures/misfires, product consumption, incidents, lessons learned. It serves for continuous improvement and as a legal record.

Step by step

  1. 1. Open the project folder: Create the event's folder (physical and digital) and list all the necessary documents/licenses with their deadlines.
  2. 2. Request licenses in advance: File with the fire department and authorities respecting the deadlines; follow up until issuance.
  3. 3. Produce the briefing, script and technical rider: Document the client's wishes, translate them into cues and list the technical needs in the rider for the production.
  4. 4. Define the crew and align with the brigade: Assign roles, train the crew and agree with the brigade/fire department on presence, signals and incident response.
  5. 5. Check everything on the day and file it: On the day, confirm that all licenses and documents are available; after the show, produce the post-event report and file it.

In practice: For a wedding with indoor effects, the operator opens the project folder, requests the fire-department inspection 15 days in advance, builds the technical rider (needs two power panels, two truss points and a time code signal from the DJ) and agrees with the venue's brigade that the smoke will be used in specific cues so as not to trigger the alarm. On the day, all licenses are in the folder; without them, the effect would not be authorized.

Summary

  • Documentation and licenses are a legal precondition: without them, there is no show.
  • Briefing, script and technical rider organize the client's wishes and the effect's needs.
  • The brigade and fire department are essential partners and may veto effects — coordinate early.

a3fee94a-c12a-4ace-a1d3-5b375bef0641.titulo: Show simulator: from briefing to teardown, step by step
a3fee94a-c12a-4ace-a1d3-5b375bef0641.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Now we bring it all together: we will plan and execute an effect from start to finish, as in a real event.

This is the synthesis lesson: a 'simulator' that goes through all the phases of an effect — briefing, licensing, design, setup, testing, firing and teardown — with a safety checklist at each stage. Use it as a mental script for any real operation, from the small indoor effect to the big outdoor show.

  • Phase 1 — Briefing and concept: Capture the client's wishes (moments, music, intensity, identity), survey the venue and its constraints (indoor/outdoor, ceiling height, audience, typical wind). Define the effect's objectives and filter out what is feasible and safe. Output: concept and preliminary list of effects.
  • Phase 2 — Design and documentation: Turn the concept into a cue script (with calculated delays and distances), choose the products and categories suited to the environment, build the technical rider, gather licenses and safety/emergency plans. Output: approved design and complete documentation.
  • Phase 3 — Logistics and storage: Ensure the provenance of the products (authorized supplier), transport and storage according to the standard (initiators kept separate), and organize the team and PPE. Output: material and team ready and legal.
  • Phase 4 — Technical setup: Inspect the terrain; mark out the zones and distances; secure racks/holders and mortars tilted away from the audience; position CO2/smoke/sparks with clearance; install the console in a safe spot; connect and LABEL the circuits. Keep the system disarmed. Output: system set up and safe.
  • Phase 5 — Testing and rehearsal: Run the continuity test channel by channel; correct faults; align time code (if any) and do a simul-firing to validate synchronization and delays. Coordinate with the fire brigade/firefighters. Output: system tested and team aligned.
  • Phase 6 — Firing: Check the weather (wind/rain) and the cancellation triggers; isolate the firing zone; confirm 'stage clear' with the team; arm the console only then; operate the cues (manual or time code) with the abort always accessible; monitor continuously. Output: show executed safely.
  • Phase 7 — Teardown and post-event: Assess the area (look for misfires and unfired products — respect the waiting time!); disarm and unload the system; separate initiators; collect residue/fallout; pack and transport whatever is left according to the standard; produce the post-event report. Output: site clean, material stored, record made.
  • The mindset of the complete operator: At every phase, ask: is it safe for audience, team, artists and venue? Do I have documentation? Do I have a plan B (contingency)? Can I abort at any moment? If the answer to any of these is 'no', stop and fix it before proceeding.

Equipment / details

  • Printed checklist and cue sheet: Operational document used in setup and firing, with each cue, circuit, product, delay and responsible person. It reduces human error and organizes the operation.
